pattern: Since every processor needs exactly the same number of tasks, handing the largest remaining group of tasks to whichever processor frees up earliest, and repeating that for the next-largest group and the next-available processor, always keeps the overall finish time as small as possible.
hints: A processor's finish time is its own start time plus the longest task in whatever group of four it is given, so only the single largest task per processor actually matters.

Line processors up from earliest to latest available, and line tasks up from largest to smallest; the processor that becomes free first can absorb the biggest tasks with the least damage to the overall finish time.
Hand the earliest-available processor the four largest remaining tasks, the next-earliest processor the next four largest, and so on; the answer is the worst of those per-processor finish times.

edge_inputs.description: a single processor taking its four tasks
two processors available at the same instant with identical tasks
two processors both available at time zero with varied tasks
processors available at the maximum constraint value with the smallest tasks
one dominant task that must be paired with the earlier-available processor to avoid a worse finish time
two outsized tasks that should be grouped onto the earlier processor rather than spread across both
processors with a modest gap and one clear standout task among otherwise small ones

stress.description: many processors and tasks drawn uniformly at random across the full ranges
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 25000))
    processor_time = [rng.randint(0, 10 ** 9) for _ in range(n)]
    tasks = [rng.randint(1, 10 ** 9) for _ in range(4 * n)]
    return [processor_time, tasks]

stress.description: identical processor availability with a handful of huge outlier tasks scattered among small ones
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 25000))
    processor_time = [0] * n
    tasks = [rng.randint(1, 1000) for _ in range(4 * n)]
    for i in range(n):
        tasks[i] = 10 ** 9
    rng.shuffle(tasks)
    return [processor_time, tasks]
